rcontroll practical work - Day 3

Hands-on TROLL advanced usage

Sylvain Schmitt

CIRAD

Apr 8, 2026

Menu

Introduction

Typically, running TROLL simulations starts well before TROLL or rcontroll is opened, involving numerous stages of data gathering and preparation, and continues far beyond a single model run. It may also include evaluation, sensitivity analysis, calibration, or complex virtual experimental simulation schemes.

R Environment

During this practical session, we will use the TROLL model (Maréchaux and Chave 2017) with the rcontrol package (Schmitt et al. 2023). We will also use the tidyverse packages for table manipulation and graph creation. Download them (prerequisite!):

install.packages("tidyverse", "rcontroll")

Load them with:

library(tidyverse)
library(rcontroll)
library(ggfortify)

Prepare Species

Perhaps the most fastidious task is gathering all the species information.

Functional trait data

Possible sources:

  • local / study site
  • new measurements
  • regional / nearby sites
  • public datasets from articles
  • public database / TRY (Kattge et al. 2019)

Example (Vleminckx et al. 2021)

ft <- readxl::read_xlsx("data/day3/oik13191-sup-0002-appendixs2.xlsx",
                        "App.S6-ok", skip = 3) %>%
  rename(s_name = Species, s_wsg = WSG) %>% 
  mutate(s_LMA = 1/SLA*1000, s_Nmass = N/100, s_Pmass = P/10^6) %>% 
  filter(s_LMA < 200) %>% 
  select(s_name, s_LMA, s_Nmass, s_Pmass, s_wsg)

Example (Vleminckx et al. 2021)

autoplot(princomp(select(ft, -s_name), cor = TRUE),
         alpha = 0.25, col = "lightgrey", loadings.label.size = 6,
         loadings.label.colour = 'red', loadings.label.vjust = 1.1,
         loadings.label.repel   = TRUE,
         loadings = T, loadings.label = T, loadings.colour = 'red') + theme_bw()

Species scaling data

Possible sources:

  • local / study site diameter and height inventories
  • new field measurements or lidar data
  • regional / nearby sites diameter and height inventories
  • public datasets from articles
  • public database / TALLO (Jucker et al. 2022)

Example (Jucker et al. 2022)

tallo <- read_tsv('data/day3/tallo_amazonia.tsv') %>% 
  filter(longitude <= -39 & longitude >= -79 & latitude >= -18 & latitude <= 10) %>% 
  filter(!is.na(species)) %>% 
  select(species, stem_diameter_cm, height_m)

Example (Jucker et al. 2022)

tallo
# A tibble: 8,363 × 3
   species                stem_diameter_cm height_m
   <chr>                             <dbl>    <dbl>
 1 Licania latistipula                 5        7.4
 2 Licania parviflora                  5        9.5
 3 Ouratea melinonii                   5.1      7.5
 4 Casearia sylvestris                 5.1      7  
 5 Rinorea flavescens                  5.1      4.9
 6 Cassipourea guianensis              5.1      7.4
 7 Rinorea flavescens                  5.1      5.4
 8 Sagotia racemosa                    5.1      7.2
 9 Sagotia racemosa                    5.1     10  
10 Mouriri crassifolia                 5.1      9.2
# ℹ 8,353 more rows

Example (Jucker et al. 2022)

tallo %>% group_by(species) %>% filter(n() > 30) %>% 
  summarise(s_dbhmax = quantile(stem_diameter_cm, .95)) %>% 
  ggplot(aes(s_dbhmax)) + geom_histogram() + theme_bw()

Example (Jucker et al. 2022)

ggplot(tallo, aes(stem_diameter_cm, height_m)) +
  geom_point(aes(col = species), alpha = 0.25) +
  theme_bw() + geom_smooth() + scale_color_discrete(guide = "none")

Example (Jucker et al. 2022)

Next we need to infer the Michaelis Menten model:

\[ \begin{array}{c} log(height) \sim N [ log(h_{max,s} \times \frac{dbh}{a_{h,s}+dbh^2}), \sigma^2] \\ h_{max,s} \sim N (h_{max}, \sigma_h^2) \\ a_{h,s} \sim N (a_{h}, \sigma_a^2) \end{array} \]

Example (Jucker et al. 2022)

For instance using brms:

fit <- brm(bf(log(h) ~ log(hmax * (d/ (ah + d))), 
              hmax ~ 1 + 1|species,
              ah ~ 1 + 1|species,
              nl = TRUE), 
           prior = c(
             prior(normal(40, 10), lb = 10, nlpar = "hmax"),
             prior(normal(0.5, 0.5), lb = 0, nlpar = "ah")
             ),
           data = mdata, chains = 2, cores = 2, threads = 10)

Imputation

Often, some species data are missing. TROLL doesn’t support it, which requires species removal or data imputation. I suggest striking a balance between keeping species diversity and a diversity of functional strategies represented (e.g. including pioneers), while trying to limit the amount of “false data” imputed. Technically, we can use extra information from other correlated traits or phylogenetic distances if we expect phylogenetic conservatism. In terms of methods and tools, I used to rely on multivariate imputation by chained equations from the R mice package. However, I now rely on machine learning with random forest for imputation using missForest.

Prepare Climate

Extensive climate data are also needed.

Sources

We need half-hourly variations and thus require data with high temporal resolution. Possible sources includes:

  • local weather station and or flux tower (but beware as not mandatorily standard)
  • nearby weather station and or flux tower
  • global datasets, e.g. FLUXNET2015 (Pastorello et al. 2020)
  • global reanalyses, as ERA5-Land (Muñoz-Sabater et al. 2021)

Our better a combination for evaluation and debiasing of global high resolution products against local data.

Sources

  • Local weather station needs contacts or might be public data as MeteoFrance
  • Global datasets and reanalyses can be accessed online through Google Earth Engine or the Climate Data Store
  • rcontroll includes a vignette and functions to get data from ERA5-Land and format them for usage in TROLL: https://sylvainschmitt.github.io/rcontroll/articles/climate.html

For ERA5-Land both for TROLL 3 or TROLL 4, I developed a python script for efficient data gathering on a given area https://github.com/sylvainschmitt/troll_fg/blob/main/data/raw_data/era/get_era_ee.py

Example ERA5-Land

system.file("extdata",
    "ERA5land_mth_Nouragues_2021_2022.nc",
    package = "rcontroll"
) %>% terra::rast()
class       : SpatRaster 
size        : 3, 3, 336  (nrow, ncol, nlyr)
resolution  : 0.1005001, 0.0999999  (x, y)
extent      : -52.90525, -52.60375, 3.911, 4.211  (xmin, xmax, ymin, ymax)
coord. ref. : lon/lat WGS 84 (CRS84) (OGC:CRS84) 
sources     : ERA5land_mth_Nouragues_2021_2022.nc:u10  (48 layers) 
              ERA5land_mth_Nouragues_2021_2022.nc:v10  (48 layers) 
              ERA5land_mth_Nouragues_2021_2022.nc:d2m  (48 layers) 
              ... and 4 more sources
varnames    : u10 (10 metre U wind component) 
              v10 (10 metre V wind component) 
              d2m (2 metre dewpoint temperature) 
              ...
names       : u10_e~r=1_1, u10_e~r=5_1, u10_e~r=1_2, u10_e~r=5_2, u10_e~r=1_3, u10_e~r=5_3, ... 
unit        :     m s**-1,     m s**-1,     m s**-1,     m s**-1,     m s**-1,     m s**-1, ... 
depth       : 1 to 5 (: 2 steps) 
time        : 2021-01-01 to 2022-12-01 UTC (24 steps)

Example ERA5-Land

climate <- generate_climate(
  x = -52.75468,
  y = 4.060414,
  tz = "America/Cayenne",
  era5land_hour = system.file("extdata", "ERA5land_hr_Nouragues_2022.nc",
    package = "rcontroll"
  ),
  era5land_month = system.file("extdata", "ERA5land_mth_Nouragues_2021_2022.nc",
    package = "rcontroll"
  )
)

Example ERA5-Land

climate$climatedaytime12
# A tibble: 12 × 12
   Temperature DaytimeMeanTemperature NightTemperature Rainfall WindSpeed
         <dbl>                  <dbl>            <dbl>    <dbl>     <dbl>
 1        23.8                   25.2             23.3    19.2      1.30 
 2        23.6                   25.0             23.1    42.4      1.22 
 3        24.0                   25.4             23.5    36.3      1.38 
 4        24.3                   25.8             23.8    30.4      1.26 
 5        24.3                   25.8             23.8    45.8      0.983
 6        24.0                   25.4             23.5    42.5      0.561
 7        24.5                   25.9             23.9    34.6      0.578
 8        25.2                   26.7             24.7     9.73     0.848
 9        26.1                   27.6             25.5     4.68     1.11 
10        26.3                   27.9             25.7     7.66     1.39 
11        24.8                   26.3             24.3    18.0      1.13 
12        24.2                   25.6             23.6    38.0      1.18 
# ℹ 7 more variables: DaytimeMeanIrradiance <dbl>, MeanIrradiance <dbl>,
#   SaturatedVapourPressure <dbl>, VapourPressure <dbl>,
#   VaporPressureDeficit <dbl>, DayTimeVapourPressureDeficitVPDbasic <dbl>,
#   DaytimeMeanVapourPressureDeficit <dbl>

Example ERA5-Land

climate$daytimevar
   starttime endtime vardaytime_light vardaytime_vpd vardaytime_T
1        6.5     7.0       0.14139434     0.06585713    0.8487613
2        7.0     7.5       0.28363396     0.08120617    0.8535856

16      14.0    14.5       1.42212641     1.70546426    1.0888685
17      14.5    15.0       1.22226487     1.68589884    1.0850613

19      15.5    16.0       0.81676441     1.44498705    1.0578410
20      16.0    16.5       0.63853409     1.25884357    1.0380195
21      16.5    17.0       0.45159493     1.10617038    1.0209843
22      17.0    17.5       0.27218105     0.97326296    1.0055368

24      18.0    18.5       0.02966932     0.72254586    0.9736733

Prepare Soil

Soil data needs to be informed in TROLL 4.

Sources

We need at least textures, and if possible SOC, DBD, CEC, pH:

  • local or nearby soil analyses
  • global datasets, e.g. SoilGrids (Poggio et al. 2021)

Depending on the area I’m really not confident on SoilGrids, e.g. in French Guiana with only a few swamp samples over-extrapolated.

Example (Schmitt et al. 2025)

Gathered by J. Lischtein for two sites required multiple references:

  • Textures for Paracou (Van Langenhove et al. (2021): Table 1, slope SLD soils, 0-30 cm and Gourlet-Fleury et al. (2004): Table 3, deep ferralitic soils, 0-40 cm) and Tapajos (Silver et al. (2000): Table 5, gradient position 350).
  • SOC for Paracou (Van Langenhove et al. (2021): mean of slope SLD soil values (0-30 cm depth) in Tables 2-3, dry and wet seasons averaged) and Tapajos (Quesada et al. (2010): Table S2, 0-30 cm depth).
  • DBD for Paracou (Van Langenhove et al. (2021): mean over 0-30 cm depth for superficial lateral drainage (SLD) slope soils) and Tapajos (Silver et al. (2000): mean for clay soils (0-10 cm depth) from Table 1, gradient positions 350 and 400).
  • pH for Paracou (Van Langenhove et al. (2021): mean of slope SLD soil values 0-30 cm depth in Table 1) and Tapajos (Quesada et al. (2010): Table S2, 0-30 cm).
  • CEC for Paracou (Sabatier et al. (1997): Table 2, SLD soils, 0-30 cm) and Tapajos (Quesada et al. (2010): Table S2, 0-30 cm depth).
  • Soil depth in metre (Nepstad et al. 2002) for Tapajos.

LIDAR simulation

TROLL 3.8 introduces lidar simulation, expanding the range of outputs and comparisons available. https://sylvainschmitt.github.io/rcontroll/articles/lidar.html

LIDAR module parameters

generate_lidar(mean_beam_pc = 10, iter_pointcloud_generation = 11) %>% 
  knitr::kable()
param value description
mean_beam_pc 10.00 /* mean pulse density (pulses per m2) */
sd_beam_pc 5.00 /* sd of pulse density (per m2) */
klaser_pc 0.63 /* the k constant (reflecting geometric properties of the canopy) */
transmittance_laser 0.40 /* percentage of pulses that continue through the canopy after a hit, multiply with klaser_pc to obtain an effective klaser */
iter_pointcloud_generation 11.00 /* iteration for point cloud generation */

LIDAR module usage

sim <- troll(
  name = "test",
  global = update_parameters(TROLLv3_output, nbiter = 12 * 1),
  species = TROLLv3_output@inputs$species,
  climate = TROLLv3_output@inputs$climate,
  daily = TROLLv3_output@inputs$daily,
  forest = get_forest(TROLLv3_output),
  lidar = generate_lidar(mean_beam_pc = 10, iter_pointcloud_generation = 11),
  verbose = TRUE
)

LIDAR module usage

sim
Object of class : trollsim 
Name : test 
Path :  
In memory : TRUE 
Extended : TRUE 
From data : FALSE 
Lidar simulation : TRUE 
Random : FALSE 

2D discrete network: horizontal step =  1 m, one tree per 1 m^2 
Number of sites      :  100 x 100 
Number of iterations :  12 
Duration of timestep :  30.41667 days 
Number of Species    :  45

LIDAR module output

sim@las
[[1]]
class        : LAS (v1.2 format 0)
memory       : 5 Mb 
extent       : 0, 100, 0, 100 (xmin, xmax, ymin, ymax)
coord. ref.  : NA 
area         : 10020 units²
points       : 144.9 thousand points
density      : 14.46 points/units²
density      : 9.58 pulses/units²

LIDAR plot

lidR::plot(sim@las[[1]])

Canopy height model

get_chm(sim)
$test
class       : SpatRaster 
size        : 102, 102, 1  (nrow, ncol, nlyr)
resolution  : 1, 1  (x, y)
extent      : -1, 101, -1, 101  (xmin, xmax, ymin, ymax)
coord. ref. :  
source(s)   : memory
name        : canopy_height 
min value   :       4.57000 
max value   :      34.00222

Canopy height plot

rcontroll::autoplot(sim, what = "lidar")

Model evaluation

The typical use of any new site, version, development or process implies model evaluation (not validation).

Real data

We will use Plot 6 Paracou data from 2016 freely available in LoggingLab (Badouard et al. 2024):

load("data/day3/Paracou6_2016.rda")
Paracou6_2016

Forest structure - real

dist_par <- Paracou6_2016 %>% 
  mutate(dbh = CircCorr/pi) %>% 
  mutate(dbh_class = cut(dbh, 
                         breaks = c(seq(10, 155, by = 5)), 
                         labels = c(seq(10, 150, by = 5)))) %>% 
  group_by(dbh_class) %>% 
  summarise(abundance = n()/6.25) %>% 
  mutate(dbh_class = as.numeric(as.character(dbh_class)))

Forest structure - real

ggplot(dist_par, aes(dbh_class, abundance)) + geom_line() + theme_bw()

Forest structure - virtual

dist_troll <- TROLLv3_output@forest %>% 
  mutate(dbh = dbh*100) %>% 
  mutate(dbh_class = cut(dbh, 
                         breaks = c(seq(10, 155, by = 5)), 
                         labels = c(seq(10, 150, by = 5)))) %>% 
  group_by(dbh_class) %>% 
  summarise(abundance = n()/1) %>% 
  mutate(dbh_class = as.numeric(as.character(dbh_class))) %>% 
  na.omit()

Forest structure - virtual

ggplot(dist_par, aes(dbh_class, abundance)) + geom_line() + theme_bw() +
  geom_line(data = dist_troll, col = "lightblue")

Forest structure - comparison

dist_par %>% rename(abundance_par = abundance) %>% left_join(dist_troll) %>% 
  ggplot(aes(abundance, abundance_par, col = dbh_class)) + geom_abline() +
  geom_point() + theme_bw() + scale_color_viridis_c()

Forest structure - simulation time

rcontroll::autoplot(TROLLv3_output)

Forest structure - longer simulation

⚠️ Long!

sim <- troll(
  name = "test",
  global = generate_parameters(
    cols = 100, rows = 100, nbiter = 12 * 600
  ),
  species = TROLLv3_species,
  climate = TROLLv3_climatedaytime12,
  daily = TROLLv3_daytimevar
)
dist_troll <- sim@forest %>% 
  mutate(dbh = dbh*100) %>% 
  mutate(dbh_class = cut(dbh, 
                         breaks = c(seq(10, 155, by = 5)), 
                         labels = c(seq(10, 150, by = 5)))) %>% 
  group_by(dbh_class) %>% 
  summarise(abundance = n()/1) %>% 
  mutate(dbh_class = as.numeric(as.character(dbh_class))) %>% 
  na.omit()

Forest structure - virtual

ggplot(dist_par, aes(dbh_class, abundance)) + geom_line() + theme_bw() +
  geom_line(data = dist_troll, col = "lightblue")

Forest structure - comparison

dist_par %>% rename(abundance_par = abundance) %>% left_join(dist_troll) %>% 
  ggplot(aes(abundance, abundance_par, col = dbh_class)) + geom_abline() +
  geom_point() + theme_bw() + scale_color_viridis_c()

Sensitivity analysis

To better understand the behaviour of the model or the parameters to be calibrated, sensitivity analyses are required. ⚠️ Computing will be heavier.

Parameter of interest

For instance:

m Minimal death rate

As a strong impact on dynamic and structure.

Simulation plan

m <- c(0.003, 0.008, 0.013, 0.018, 0.023)
names(m) <- paste0("m=", m)
stack_pars <- lapply(m, function(m)
  generate_parameters(cols = 100, rows = 100, nbiter = 12 * 600, m = m, m1 = m)) %>% 
  bind_rows(.id = "simulation")

Simulation plan

filter(stack_pars, param == "m") 
  simulation param value              description
1    m=0.003     m 0.003 /* minimal death rate */
2    m=0.008     m 0.008 /* minimal death rate */
3    m=0.013     m 0.013 /* minimal death rate */
4    m=0.018     m 0.018 /* minimal death rate */
5    m=0.023     m 0.023 /* minimal death rate */

Simulations

⚠️ Long & heavy !!

stack <- stack(
  name = "test",
  simulations = names(m),
  global = stack_pars,
  species = TROLLv3_species,
  climate = TROLLv3_climatedaytime12,
  daily = TROLLv3_daytimevar,
  verbose = FALSE
) 

  |                                                                            
  |                                                                      |   0%
  |                                                                            
  |======================================================================| 100%

Simulations

stack 
Object of class : trollstack 
Name : test 
Path :  
In memory : TRUE 
Number of simulations : 5 
Extended : TRUE 
From data : FALSE 
Lidar simulation : FALSE 
Random : FALSE 

2D discrete network: horizontal step =  1 m, one tree per 1 m^2 
Number of sites      :  100 x 100 
Number of iterations :  7200 
Duration of timestep :  30.41667 days 
Number of Species    :  45

Simulations

rcontroll::autoplot(stack, what = "temporal", var = "agb")

Sensitivity test

agb <- stack@forest %>% group_by(simulation) %>% summarise(agb = sum(AGB))
sensitivity::pcc(data.frame(m = unname(m), intercept = 0), agb$agb) 

Call:
sensitivity::pcc(X = data.frame(m = unname(m), intercept = 0),     y = agb$agb)

Partial Correlation Coefficients (PCC):
            original
m         -0.9624372
intercept         NA

Point of attention

  • Equifinality

  • Parameters links

Calibration analysis

Some parameters cannot be easily informed by physical or ecological factors and require calibration. ⚠️ Computing will be heavier.

Parameter of interest

For instance:

m Minimal death rate

As a strong impact on dynamic and structure.

Expectation

In Paracou, Aboveground live biomass was 197.5-221.5 Mg C/ha in 2007 (Rutishauser et al. 2010).

Simulations

rcontroll::autoplot(stack, what = "temporal", var = "agb") + 
  geom_hline(yintercept = 2*c(197.5, 221.5)*10^3, col = "red", linetype = "dashed")

Point of attention

  • Equifinality

  • Parameters links

Schmitt et al. 2020

Let’s try to reproduce a reduced version of a “real life” example with Schmitt et al. (2020).

Warning

⚠️ Computing will be heavier and very long. Might be limited for the workshop.

Link

PDF

References

Badouard, Vincyane, Sylvain Schmitt, Guillaume Salzet, Thomas Gaquiere, Margaux Rojat, Caroline Bedeau, Olivier Brunaux, and Géraldine Derroire. 2024. “LoggingLab: An R Package to Simulate Reduced-Impact Selective Logging in Tropical Forests Using Forest Inventory Data.” Ecological Modelling 487 (January): 110539. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecolmodel.2023.110539.
Jucker, Tommaso, Fabian Jörg Fischer, Jérôme Chave, David A. Coomes, John Caspersen, Arshad Ali, Grace Jopaul Loubota Panzou, et al. 2022. “Tallo: A Global Tree Allometry and Crown Architecture Database.” Global Change Biology 28 (17): 5254–68. https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.16302.
Kattge, Jens, Gerhard Bönisch, Sandra Díaz, Sandra Lavorel, Iain Colin Prentice, Paul Leadley, Susanne Tautenhahn, et al. 2019. “TRY Plant Trait Database Enhanced Coverage and Open Access.” Global Change Biology 26 (1): 119–88. https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.14904.
Maréchaux, Isabelle, and Jérôme Chave. 2017. “An Individual-Based Forest Model to Jointly Simulate Carbon and Tree Diversity in Amazonia: Description and Applications.” Ecological Monographs 87: 632–64. https://doi.org/10.1002/ecm.1271.
Muñoz-Sabater, Joaquín, Emanuel Dutra, Anna Agustí-Panareda, Clément Albergel, Gabriele Arduini, Gianpaolo Balsamo, Souhail Boussetta, et al. 2021. “ERA5-Land: A State-of-the-Art Global Reanalysis Dataset for Land Applications.” Earth System Science Data 13 (9): 4349–83. https://doi.org/10.5194/essd-13-4349-2021.
Pastorello, Gilberto, Carlo Trotta, Eleonora Canfora, Housen Chu, Danielle Christianson, You-Wei Cheah, Cristina Poindexter, et al. 2020. “The FLUXNET2015 Dataset and the ONEFlux Processing Pipeline for Eddy Covariance Data.” Scientific Data 7 (1). https://doi.org/10.1038/s41597-020-0534-3.
Poggio, Laura, Luis M. de Sousa, Niels H. Batjes, Gerard B. M. Heuvelink, Bas Kempen, Eloi Ribeiro, and David Rossiter. 2021. “SoilGrids 2.0: Producing Soil Information for the Globe with Quantified Spatial Uncertainty.” SOIL 7 (1): 217–40. https://doi.org/10.5194/soil-7-217-2021.
Schmitt, Sylvain, Fabian J. Fischer, James G. C. Ball, Nicolas Barbier, Marion Boisseaux, Damien Bonal, Benoit Burban, et al. 2025. “TROLL 4.0: Representing Water and Carbon Fluxes, Leaf Phenology, and Intraspecific Trait Variation in a Mixed-Species Individual-Based Forest Dynamics Model Part 2: Model Evaluation for Two Amazonian Sites.” Geoscientific Model Development 18 (16): 5205–43. https://doi.org/10.5194/gmd-18-5205-2025.
Schmitt, Sylvain, Isabelle Maréchaux, Jerome Chave, Fabian Jörg Fischer, Camille Piponiot, Stéphane Traissac, and Bruno Hérault. 2020. “Functional Diversity Improves Tropical Forest Resilience: Insights from a Long-Term Virtual Experiment.” Edited by Lorena Gomez-Aparicio. Journal of Ecology 108 (3): 831–43. https://doi.org/10.1111/1365-2745.13320.
Schmitt, Sylvain, Guillaume Salzet, Fabian Jörg Fischer, Isabelle Maréchaux, and Jerome Chave. 2023. “Rcontroll: An r Interface for the Individual-Based Forest Dynamics Simulator TROLL.” Methods in Ecology and Evolution 14 (11): 2749–57. https://doi.org/10.1111/2041-210X.14215.
Vleminckx, Jason, Claire Fortunel, Oscar Valverde-Barrantes, C. E. Timothy Paine, Julien Engel, Pascal Petronelli, Aurélie K. Dourdain, Juan Guevara, Solène Béroujon, and Christopher Baraloto. 2021. “Resolving Whole-Plant Economics from Leaf, Stem and Root Traits of 1467 Amazonian Tree Species.” Oikos 130 (7): 1193–1208. https://doi.org/10.1111/oik.08284.
